nativeRange.habitat: Coral and silty reef areas, lagoons, and seaward reefs at depths of approximately 4–60 meters; tends to favor areas with mixed coral and rubble rather than dense coral growth

diet.notes: Has been documented eating algae, sponges, and small invertebrates in the wild. In aquariums, generally advised to offer a varied diet including spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations containing sponge matter, mysis shrimp, and other meaty foods. Feeding 2–3 times per day tends to support good health and coloration.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 3164x2109; sharp in-situ Red Sea reef shot of adult showing classic slate-blue body and bold yellow bar, surrounded by anthias - most striking and representative.
Diego Delso; 7321x4881; crisp full-body profile against dark background making the deep blue and yellow pop; different individual and setting for variety.
Diego Delso; 3514x2343; sharp overhead-angle shot over sandy bottom, whole fish in frame, clear view of scale pattern and orange-yellow bar.

summary: A large angelfish from the Red Sea and western Indian Ocean, generally considered best suited to very large systems. Juveniles display vivid blue and white barring; adults develop a bold yellow vertical bar with a transparent yellowish tail.
careNotes:
The Maculosus Angelfish is among the larger angelfish kept in home aquariums, commonly reaching 40–50 cm in captivity, with a reported maximum of 50 cm and a wild lifespan of up to 36 years. A single specimen per tank is generally advised; the species tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly shaped fish.

Reef safety: Sources are divided on this point. In practice, individuals vary considerably — some keepers have reported successful cohabitation with less palatable corals, but the species tends to pick at sessile invertebrates (animals that remain fixed in place, such as corals and clams). Caution is warranted in reef setups.

The intermediate care level reflects the species' large adult size and substantial tank requirements of at least around 700 liters. The fish itself is generally considered hardy once established and tends to accept prepared foods readily, but total system demands are higher than for many marine fish.
breedingNotes: Pomacanthids are sequential hermaphrodites and are generally considered extremely difficult to breed in captivity. Captive breeding of P. maculosus has not been reliably documented; wild-caught specimens are the norm in the trade.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm SL), temperature range (24.8–29.3°C), native range, taxonomy, and lifespan (up to 36 years reported). Temperature minimum notably higher than LiveAquaria (24.8°C vs 22.2°C).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Third cross-check source. Confirmed size 50 cm, temperature 24–28°C, salinity SG 1.023–1.026. Rated care level 'moderate to difficult' (vs LiveAquaria 'easy') and reef safe 'no' (vs LiveAquaria 'with caution'). Tank minimum given as 500 L, lower than LiveAquaria's 250 gal (946 L). Used in averaging temperature, salinity, tank volume, and care level.

fish.breedingNotes: Pomacanthids are sequential hermaphrodites; captive breeding of P. maculosus has not been reliably documented. Wild-caught specimens are standard in the aquarium trade.
